Current Focus

What I'm building

01

Next-Gen Recruiting Dashboards

Rebuilding legacy People Analytics into a self-service platform on Databricks and Sigma — millions of records, firm-wide scope. The goal isn't better reports; it's a system people can use without asking.

02

Career Coaching at Leland

Career strategy for professionals navigating pivots and growth. Now expanding into Leland's Break into AI Careers category — coaching people through the same transition I'm living.

03

AI Workflow Automation

Building automated systems with Claude and connected tools — compressing analyst and recruiting workflows from hours into minutes.

04

Building & Documenting in Public

Every project ships with documentation: what I built, how I built it, and the exact prompts used. As useful for others as it is for me.

Built with AI · Leland 2026 AI Builder Program

How I Built This
Design System Skill
01
Found an inspiration website
Found a site that looked exactly how I wanted mine to feel: serious, editorial, the kind of thing that belongs somewhere expensive. That was the only reference I needed.
02
Used Claude in Chrome to extract the design DNA
Pointed Claude at the inspiration site and asked it to document the full design system: color palette with exact hex codes, typeface names and weights, spacing values, layout principles, and brand vibe. Everything extracted from the live site. No guessing.
03
Used /skill-creator to turn it into a reusable skill
Packaged the extracted design system into a structured SKILL.md file using the skill creator. The file becomes a set of instructions Claude reads automatically on every invocation: colors, type specs, layout rules, and brand vibe all baked in.
04
Now it works everywhere: /abc-design
Every build that invokes the skill automatically inherits the full design system. No re-explaining the brand, no drift between outputs — it just works.
Documentation
Design System Skill
Color Palette
Brand Copper
#8F5A39
Prestige accent — once per section
Deep Navy
#27455C
Structure, headers, hero bg
Interactive Blue
#2670A9
Links, buttons, CTAs
Warm Cream
#F4EFE7
Default page background
Near-Black
#202020
All body text
White
#FFFFFF
Cards and content panels
Typography
Display / Headings — Cormorant Garamond, Light 300
The measured authority of editorial design.
Sub-headings / Body — Crimson Pro, Light 300
Every design decision should feel like it belongs in a boardroom or a financial magazine. Restraint is strength. Generous space is a brand value, not a luxury.
UI / Labels / Buttons — Open Sans, SemiBold 600
Navigation · Buttons · Labels · Metadata
Type Scale
72 48 36 24 16 12
Layout Principles
  • Full-bleed heroes — edge-to-edge sections, centered max-width container for content
  • 100px vertical rhythm — generous section spacing; crowding breaks the brand
  • Border-radius: 2px — near-square corners on all buttons and UI elements, never rounded
  • Copper rules — dividers and accent borders use Brand Copper, never generic gray
  • White cards on cream — surfaces sit slightly elevated above the background field
Brand Vibe
Authoritative Editorial Measured Institutional Prestige

Every decision should pass this filter: does it belong in The Economist, a Goldman Sachs annual report, or a museum exhibition? If yes, proceed. If it feels startup-y or trendy, pull back.

How I Built This
Portfolio Website
01
Started with a simple scaffolded HTML page
Asked Claude to build a single-file portfolio with the sections I needed: Hero, About, Work, AI Projects grid, and Contact. No design system yet, just clean structure and placeholder content to establish the layout.
02
Applied my design system skill to restyle it
Invoked /abc-design and had Claude restyle everything — out with the dark theme, in with the full palette, type stack, and spacing system.
03
Claude interviewed me to fill in real content
Rather than writing it myself, Claude asked me questions — name, role, what each section should actually say. It wrote the copy and placed it. I just answered.
04
Built out project cards as I completed each sprint
Each AI Builder sprint produces a real project. As I finished each one, I came back and built out its card, adding the How I Built This and Documentation popups with actual content, not placeholder text.
Documentation
Portfolio Website
Techniques Used
  • Single-file HTML — entire site in one self-contained .html file, no build tools or dependencies
  • CSS custom properties — brand tokens defined once at :root, applied consistently throughout
  • Google Fonts only — Cormorant Garamond, Crimson Pro, and Open Sans via a single <link> tag
  • IntersectionObserver — scroll-triggered fade-in for cards, no animation library required
  • Pre-baked modal content — each popup's HTML lives in the file and is swapped in via JS, no server needed
  • /abc-design skill — enforced brand consistency across every element without manual checking
  • AI-written copy — About section and project descriptions written by Claude from a short interview
Key Prompts — Build Your Own
1. Scaffold the structure
"Build me a single-page portfolio HTML file. Interview me about any info you don't have. Sections: Hero with my name and title, About (conversational), Work (2–3 focus areas), AI Projects grid with cards that open popups, and a Contact footer. Google Fonts only."
2. Apply your design system
"Use /abc-design to restyle my portfolio. Google Fonts are the only allowed external resource. Replace the current theme with my brand system — colors, typography, spacing rules, and button styles."
3. Build out a project card
"Build out the [Project Name] card in my portfolio. 'How I Built This' popup: [describe your steps]. 'Documentation' popup: [describe what to show]. Keep all other cards as Coming Soon with empty popups."
4. Create your design system skill first
"Use /skill-creator to turn this design system into a skill I can reuse everywhere. Call it [name]. No evals needed — just write the skill and package it as a .skill file I can install in Cowork and Chat."
What I Built
Email Manager
How The System Works
📥 Gmail Inbox
All unread messages pulled via MCP
🤖 AI Triage Engine
email-manager skill + humanize-writing skill + voice guide
✅ Reply Now
Needs a response today. Draft is ready and matched to my voice.
📝 Draft Ready
Reply drafted and waiting in Gmail — just review and send.
🚩 Flagged
Needs attention but isn't urgent. Summarized for easy review.
📁 FYI / Archive
No action needed. Filed and out of the way.
📬 Andrew Reviews & Sends
Triaged summary · Drafted replies · Nothing missed
Tools & Skills Used
email-manager skill humanize-writing skill Email Voice Guide Gmail MCP Claude Cowork
Documentation
Email Manager
How I Built It — Step by Step
01
Connected Gmail via MCP
Used the Gmail MCP connector in Cowork to give Claude direct access to my inbox. No copy-paste, no manual exports — it reads my actual emails in real time.
02
Invoked the email-manager skill
The skill contains instructions for how to triage: what counts as urgent, how to categorize threads, what to draft vs. just flag. It reads those instructions on every run so I never have to re-explain the system.
03
Layered in the humanize-writing skill
For any email that needs a draft reply, Claude uses the humanize-writing skill to strip AI tells and make it sound like me — not like a language model filling in a template.
04
Referenced my Email Voice Guide for tone
Every draft is calibrated against my voice guide: how I open emails, how formal I am with different audiences, my sentence patterns, and what I never say. The result actually sounds like me.
05
Receives a triage summary + ready-to-send drafts
Output is a structured digest: what needs my attention, what's already drafted in Gmail, and what's been filed. I review and send — the cognitive load is almost zero.
Copyable Prompt — Build Your Own
Trigger the full email triage workflow
"Use the email-manager skill to triage my inbox. Pull my last [X] unread emails via Gmail. Categorize each as: Reply Now, Draft Ready, Flagged, or FYI/Archive. For anything in Reply Now or Draft Ready, write a reply using the humanize-writing skill and my voice guide. Give me a summary of what needs attention, then save any drafts directly to Gmail."
If you don't have a voice guide yet
"Use the email-manager skill to triage my inbox. For draft replies, write in a warm, direct, professional tone. Avoid filler phrases like 'hope this finds you well' or 'please don't hesitate.' Get to the point quickly and match the formality level of the original email."
What Makes This Different
  • Voice-matched replies — not generic AI drafts. My voice guide makes the difference.
  • Triage logic is in the skill — no re-prompting. The skill holds the rules.
  • Gmail MCP = real access — reads live emails, saves drafts directly. No clipboard gymnastics.
  • Layered skills — email-manager + humanize-writing working together. Each does one thing well.
What I Built
Email Voice Guide
The 8 Sections of the Voice Guide

Analyzed 40 sent emails. AI identified my default tone, how I adapt across contexts, and the patterns I repeat without thinking. Output: a structured reference doc any AI can use to write emails that sound like me.

Section 01
Core Identity
My default tone, the register I fall into when I'm not thinking about it, and what I value in communication.
Section 02
Registers
How I shift across contexts — with a boss, a peer, a recruiter, a client. What changes and what stays constant.
Section 03
Sentence Patterns
How I open, how I close, sentence length, rhythm, how I punctuate — the mechanical fingerprint of my writing.
Section 04
Rhetorical Moves
The persuasion tactics I reach for most — framing, bridging, the moves I make to be heard without being pushy.
Section 05
Emotional Register
Where I'm warm vs. professional, when I inject humor, how I show care without sounding performative.
Section 06
Do's & Don'ts
Specific phrases I use and phrases I never use. What makes an AI draft sound like me vs. like a template.
Section 07
Quick Reference Table
A compact lookup: scenario → tone → approach. For AI to use before drafting any email on my behalf.
Section 08
Calibration Tests
Three test prompts with expected responses. A way to verify the guide is working before using it at scale.
Documentation
Email Voice Guide
How I Built It — Step by Step
01
Pulled 40 sent emails from Gmail
Used the Gmail MCP to export my 40 most recent sent emails. These were across different recipient types — colleagues, leadership, coaches, clients — giving the analysis enough range to find real patterns instead of situational noise.
02
Fed the corpus to Claude with a structured analysis prompt
Gave Claude all 40 emails and asked it to analyze: my default tone, how I shift across contexts, sentence-level patterns, rhetorical moves, emotional register, and what I never say. The prompt specified the 8 output sections in advance so the guide would be structured and reusable — not just a summary.
03
Claude generated a structured psychological profile
The output wasn't "here are some observations." It was a formal voice guide formatted for AI consumption — each section designed to be referenced before drafting an email on my behalf. Specific. Usable. Not a description of me, but instructions for writing like me.
04
Packaged as a .docx reference document
Saved as a Word document using the docx skill so it can be uploaded as context in any Claude session. Now the email-manager skill and humanize-writing skill can both reference it — any AI draft runs through the same filter.
The Corpus
  • 40 sent emails — drawn from the last 3 months across all recipient contexts
  • Mix of formality — leadership emails, peer check-ins, client follow-ups, warm intros, cold outreach
  • No editing before analysis — raw sent mail, exactly as I wrote it. The AI needs the unpolished version to find real patterns.
Copyable Prompt — Build Your Own
Analyze your sent emails & generate a voice guide
"I'm going to paste [X] of my sent emails. Analyze them to build a structured voice guide I can give to any AI when asking it to write emails on my behalf. The guide should have 8 sections: (1) Core Identity — my default tone and communication values; (2) Registers — how I adapt across contexts like leadership, peers, clients; (3) Sentence Patterns — how I open, close, sentence length and rhythm; (4) Rhetorical Moves — persuasion and framing tactics I use most; (5) Emotional Register — where I'm warm vs. professional and how I show care; (6) Do's & Don'ts — specific phrases I use and never use; (7) Quick Reference Table — scenario to tone to approach lookup; (8) Calibration Tests — 3 test prompts with expected responses so I can verify the guide works. Be specific. This is for AI use, not for me to read about myself."
Use the guide in any email draft
"Reference my email voice guide [attach or paste it]. Write a reply to this email in my voice: [paste the email]. Match my sentence rhythm, register, and the specific patterns in the Do's & Don'ts section. Don't start with 'Hope this finds you well.' Get to the point quickly."

Content coming soon. This section will be filled in as the project is completed.

Intro

Claude Delivers It.
Genie
Analysts · In platform
→
Claude
Everyone downstream
Use Cases
Where It Fits in Recruiting BI
Early Careers
Ad-Hoc Reports
Briefings
Workflows
QBRs
What It Covers

A 5-slide Lunch & Learn on Claude's potential in the Recruiting BI space at JPMC — covering where Claude already exists in the organization, how it fits alongside our Databricks and Sigma stack, the relationship between Databricks Genie and Claude for end-user data delivery, and five concrete Recruiting BI use cases including Early Careers cohort reports and QBR briefings.

Tools Used

Claude Cowork · /abc-design skill · Playwright (screenshot capture) · pdf-lib (PDF assembly)

Documentation
AI-Powered Research Presentation
The Workflow — Research to Finished Deck
01
Identified the research question
Started with a specific problem: where do Claude's AI tools fit within Recruiting BI at JPMC, given our existing Databricks and Sigma stack? Framed it as a question worth researching — not just an opinion.
02
Used Claude to research and validate publicly
Asked Claude to research the Databricks MCP server, the Sigma MCP connector, and Databricks Genie — then validate the claims with public documentation. Claude searched GitHub and Anthropic docs to confirm what was real and available.
03
Structured findings into a slide-by-slide outline
Used my Email Voice Guide skill to keep the framing precise and direct. Claude organized the research into a 5-slide Lunch & Learn format: context, stack fit, the Genie/Claude handoff story, and use cases. One idea per slide, no CTA.
04
Applied the /abc-design skill
Invoked the custom design skill, which automatically applied the full JPMorganChase brand system: Warm Cream backgrounds, Deep Navy structure, Brand Copper accents, Cormorant Garamond headings, and Crimson Pro body text. No manual styling decisions needed.
05
Built as a single self-contained HTML file
Claude generated a fully interactive slide deck — full-viewport sections, keyboard navigation, progress bar, and speaker notes — as one HTML file saved to my working folder.
06
Exported to PDF with Playwright
Wrote a Node.js script using Playwright (headless Chromium) to capture each slide at 3× pixel density (4320×2430 px) and assemble them into a 5-page PDF using pdf-lib. Avoided page.pdf() to preserve the slide layout.
Copyable Prompt

Use this to build a similar research presentation from scratch.

Use my design skill: /abc-design Build an HTML slide deck from [your-notes-or-ascii-file]. Requirements: - Each slide is a full-viewport section (100vh) with one idea per slide - Add a progress bar and slide number indicator - Use my design system colors, typography, and layout rules - No more than 6 slides total — Lunch & Learn format, no CTA - If any slide has too much text, move detail to speaker notes Keep the output clean. Every visual element should serve the content. Save as a single self-contained HTML file.
Export to PDF Prompt
Export [filename].html to a PDF using Playwright. Run these steps in order: 1. npm install playwright pdf-lib 2. npx playwright install chromium 3. Confirm the Chromium binary downloaded successfully before continuing 4. Write a Node.js script that opens the file at 1440x810 viewport, waits 1.5 seconds for animations to settle, captures each .slide element as an individual JPEG screenshot at 3× pixel density, assembles them into a multi-page PDF using pdf-lib, and saves it as [output-name].pdf in my working directory. Do not use page.pdf() — it treats the file as a document and breaks the slide layout. Capture each slide element individually.
What I Built
AI-Powered Data Analysis of Duke, UT Austin, and UNC
One-Pager Output
What It Is

A shareable one-pager comparing Duke, UNC, and UT Austin on tuition cost vs. 10-year median earnings — built from the US Department of Education College Scorecard dataset. The headline finding: UNC ($8,994/yr) and UT Austin ($11,688/yr) produce near-Duke earnings ($72K–$75K vs. $97K) at roughly one-sixth the cost. Designed to be the kind of thing you'd actually send to someone.

Tools Used

Claude Cowork · Excel (data exploration) · /abc-design skill · Chart.js · HTML/CSS (single-file output)

Documentation
AI-Powered Data Analysis of Duke, UT Austin, and UNC
The Workflow — Data to One-Pager
01
Started with a real dataset
Pulled the US Department of Education College Scorecard dataset — fields like in-state tuition, 10-year median earnings, acceptance rate, and enrollment. Filtered it down to Duke, UNC, and UT Austin plus a national average benchmark. Uploaded the CSV to Claude and asked for an initial read: what patterns are worth surfacing, and what a non-analyst would actually find useful.
02
Used AI to find the story in the data
Rather than writing formulas manually, I prompted Claude to analyze the dataset and flag the most meaningful comparison. It landed on cost vs. 10-year earnings — and surfaced the headline: UNC ($8,994/yr) and UT Austin ($11,688/yr) produce $72K–$75K in median earnings, almost matching Duke's $97K, at roughly one-sixth the tuition. That became the central argument of the one-pager.
03
Explored the data in Excel
Took the AI's recommended breakdown into a spreadsheet. Organized tuition and earnings side-by-side for all four data points (Duke, UNC, UT Austin, National Avg), cleaned up formatting, and used it as the source of truth for the chart values. This step locked in the exact numbers before any design work started.
04
Applied design skills to produce the one-pager
Used the /abc-design skill to generate a styled HTML one-pager. Gave Claude the three stat callouts (tuition per school), the chart data (earnings vs. tuition by school), the headline narrative, and the school color system — navy for Duke, Carolina blue for UNC, burnt orange for UT Austin. It produced a fully formatted, single-file HTML document with a grouped bar chart via Chart.js.
05
Made it shareable
Saved the finished HTML file to the project folder. Opens in any browser, no server required — just send the file. The whole pipeline, from raw CSV to polished one-pager, took under an hour. The output is the kind of thing you could drop into an email or share with a high schooler making a college decision.
Copyable Prompt — Replicate This

Use these two prompts to run the same workflow on any dataset of your choice.

1. Find the story in the data
I'm uploading a dataset: [describe what it contains — rows, columns, subject]. I want to build a shareable one-pager for a non-analyst audience. Analyze the data and tell me: - The single most interesting or counterintuitive finding - The best 2–3 comparison to show in a bar chart (what vs. what) - Three stat callouts that would land with someone who doesn't read spreadsheets Don't write any code yet. Just give me the story in plain language and the exact numbers I should use.
2. Build the one-pager
Use my design skill: /abc-design Build a shareable HTML one-pager from this data summary: Headline: [one punchy sentence that states the key finding] Stat callouts (one card per subject): - [Subject A] — [metric label]: [value] - [Subject B] — [metric label]: [value] - [Subject C] — [metric label]: [value] Bar chart title: [what you're comparing] Data (label: earnings, tuition or whatever your two metrics are): - [Category A]: [value1], [value2] - [Category B]: [value1], [value2] - [Category C]: [value1], [value2] Color system: - [Subject A]: [hex or color name] - [Subject B]: [hex or color name] - [Subject C]: [hex or color name] Requirements: - Single self-contained HTML file - Grouped bar chart (Chart.js from CDN is fine) - Header with kicker label + headline + school/subject tags - Stat cards color-coded by subject to match chart bars - Footer with data source attribution - Fixed canvas size: 1200×700px
